nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef slopes and lagoons, typically at depths of 1–35 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on coral polyps, small benthic invertebrates, and filamentous algae in the wild. In captivity tends to be a shy, deliberate feeder and may initially require live foods to transition to frozen fare; finely chopped seafoods, mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, krill, cyclops, spirulina, and high-quality marine omnivore pellets have been reported to work well. Feeding 2–3 times daily is generally advised.

summary: The Sunset Butterflyfish is a peaceful South Pacific species known for its warm yellow-orange coloration and distinctive dot-and-dash pattern. It is generally considered intermediate in difficulty, owing mainly to shy initial feeding behavior, and is best kept in a mature fish-only aquarium.
careNotes:
The Sunset Butterflyfish is generally considered a peaceful species that tends to do well in fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) aquariums alongside non-aggressive tankmates. It is not reef-safe, as it has been documented feeding on coral polyps, soft corals, tubeworms, and benthic invertebrates in the wild; it should be kept away from ornamental invertebrates and corals.

Feeding can be challenging in the early weeks. This species has been reported as a shy, deliberate feeder that may require live foods — live brine shrimp or live mysis — to trigger a feeding response before transitioning to frozen and prepared fare. A varied diet including finely chopped seafoods, mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, krill, and high-quality marine pellets is generally advised to maintain long-term health.

The Sunset Butterflyfish is best kept singly or as a mated pair. It has been reported to be territorial toward conspecifics and toward similar-looking butterflyfish, so housing multiple specimens or mixing species within the family Chaetodontidae is generally not recommended. A well-established aquarium with ample live rock providing caves, crevices, and grazing surfaces is strongly advised.

In the wild, this species ranges from Papua New Guinea and the Great Barrier Reef eastward through Fiji, Samoa, and the Cook Islands to the Tuamotu Archipelago.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous and monogamous in the wild, with spawning documented to occur at dusk. Captive breeding has not been reliably documented for this species; breeding in aquaria is considered extremely difficult and essentially not achieved under typical hobby conditions.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, care level, temperament, reef safety, and minimum tank size. Tank size listed as 50 gallons vs LiveAquaria's 70 gallons and TropicalFishKeeping's 75 gallons; averaged across three sources to ~246 L.

additional.name: TropicalFishKeeping
additional.notes: Third source for cross-verification. Adult size listed as 4.75 inches; lifespan 5–8 years confirmed; tank size 75 gallons. Temperature and salinity ranges agreed with LiveAquaria.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: FishBase returned HTTP 403; taxonomy and distribution data obtained via secondary search referencing FishBase record ID 6606. Maximum length reported as 12.5 cm; native range confirmed as southern Pacific from Papua New Guinea to the Tuamotu Archipelago.

fish.breedingNotes: Reported oviparous and monogamous; spawning documented at dusk in the wild. Not reliably bred in captivity under hobby conditions.
